content.rendered:
“Like Bruce Springsteen, Texas songwriter Joe Ely crafts brawny, epic song-stories about good-hearted crooks, blue-collar heroes and enduring lovers. In fact, the primary difference between Springsteen and Ely comes down to accent'”  –Chicago Tribune, November 30, 2001

We lost the late, great Joe Ely last year, not long after Bruce Springsteen inducted him in absentia into the American Music Honors honor roll.

Fortunately, we were able to enjoy one last collaboration between the two artists before Joe’s passing.

The two singer-songwriters first appeared on record together in 1995, on Ely’s landmark album Letter to Laredo. Almost twenty years later, they reunited for Joe’s slow groove, “Odds of the Blues.”

Inspiration struck Ely one evening at “an all-night after-hours joint on the edge of east Lubbock called TV’s,” he wrote in a press release. “There was always a dice game in the back room, the pool table had a bad lean, and the jukebox mainly played old blues songs.”

Ely wrote “Odds of the Blues” to capture that TV’s vibe and asked Springsteen to join him on the track. Bruce readily accepted, and his vocals are evident throughout the track.

“We’ve been long lost friends for a long time,” recalled Ely in 2024, although his memory may have been just a little bit spotty. “One of my memories of us singing together was in Dublin, Ireland when we both got on stage with Jerry Lee Lewis and Shane MacGowan and sang ‘Great Balls of Fire.'”

Joe and Jerry Lee did join Bruce on stage in Dublin one night in 1993, but there’s no record of MacGowan having been there.

Still, who can quibble? Joe Ely was one of the all-time great Texas singer-songwriters, and he’ll be missed by anyone fortunate enough to have heard him on disc or on stage.

Odds of the Blues
Recorded: 2024
Released: Driven to Drive (2024)
Never performed

© July 8, 2026

aioseo_breadcrumb: Home » It Takes Two » Odds of the Blues

jetpack-related-posts.title: Fingernails
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Twice only: Bruce and Joe Ely jam to an extended "Fingernails" on stage in Dublin.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Blowin’ Down This Road
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Woody Guthrie's "Blowin' Down This Road" brings Bruce together with his fellow singer-songwriters. Hear him team up with Joe Ely, Joe Grushecky, Elliott Murphy, Alejando Escovedo, and Garland Jeffreys inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: I’ve Got to Know
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: A host of artists join Bruce in paying tribute to Woody Guthrie in this performance from 1996.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Oklahoma Hills
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: At a 1996 Hall of Fame tribute concert, Bruce teamed up with Joe Ely and Arlo Guthrie for Woody Guthrie's tribute to Oklahoma.

jetpack-related-posts.title: October 19
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: On this date: Rosie goes missing, Queen of the Supermarket turns up, Philly Elvis makes his debut, and more. Plus: highlights from the star-studded Alliance of Neighbors benefit concert.

jetpack-related-posts.title: October 18
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Bruce broke up the band on this date in 1989, but there are plenty of happy highlights too: the first-ever performances of "Drive All Night" and "Hungry Heart," an adorable tape-recorded message to local schoolchildren, the premiere of Legends of Springsteen, Charlie's first E Street appearance, and more.
